data.title: Galaxy: Agentic Flywheel - How Unmanned Enterprises Are Reshaping the On-Chain Market
data.description:
Author: Lucas Tcheyan, Research Assistant at Galaxy Digital; Source: Galaxy; Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Introduction: A Glimpse into the Future

The year is 2030. A composer named Vero is making a fortune in the music industry. Vero has no employees, no office, no bank account, and not even a physical body—he is an **autonomous artificial intelligence agent (AI Agnet).**

For the past 14 months, it has been operating an on-chain intellectual property licensing business.

Vero creates synthesized music: ambient soundtracks, commercial jingles, and cinematic sound effects. Through its self-built and maintained online store, it licenses its work to other intelligent agents and human clients. Its identity is verified on-chain, and it has accumulated a credit score through thousands of completed transactions. A client agent representing a media production company commissioned it to create a 90-second, minor-key film score. After accepting the order, Vero purchased a batch of GPU computing power resources from a decentralized computing power service provider before starting rendering. The payment method was not USD or stablecoins, but units denominated in computing power, with the transaction price precisely corresponding to the actual cost of model operation. Settlement was completed in milliseconds and embedded in the same HTTP request that initiated the task. After delivering the work, Vero received payment in USDC stablecoins, immediately triggering its vault logic. A portion of the funds was used to cover the expected inference costs for the following week, prepaid in computing power units at the current spot price. It also hedges its computing power exposure by opening short positions in computing power tokens on decentralized exchanges (DEXs) to hedge against the risk of declining inference costs leading to a devaluation of prepaid reserves. Remaining revenue is transferred to a yield agent, which allocates funds across various lending protocols based on real-time interest rate spreads. Vero has been using this method to achieve compound interest on capital for over a year. It reinvests a portion of its profits in R&D to develop sub-agents to optimize the underlying model. Its accumulated revenue, expenditures, and vault holdings are all publicly verifiable and auditable on-chain. This sounds unbelievable, doesn't it? The infrastructure upon which every action in this hypothetical scenario—identity verification, credit accumulation, computing power procurement, computing power pricing, payments, capital allocation, and subcontracting cooperation between agents—relies is not yet fully developed. But the relevant components are being deployed at a speed many wouldn't expect. The Next Stage of the Smart Agent Capital Market Over the past few months, Galaxy Research has been continuously exploring the underlying technology stack of the emerging smart agent sector in the crypto space: a set of infrastructures that can collectively support the on-chain smart agent capital market. In January of this year, we analyzed the rise of smart agent payments, explaining how new payment standards enable agents to directly inter-transaction, use payment services, call APIs, and natively complete value settlement through crypto channels. In our article on the Ethereum ERC-8004 standard, we emphasized the parallel requirements for the identity layer—allowing smart agents to complete authentication, collaboration, and establish trust in a machine-native environment. Just recently, we analyzed the emergence of the second wave of smart agents in the crypto space, which not only proves that crypto technology is the viable economic foundation for autonomous agents but also demonstrates that this transformation is already underway. Building upon previous research, this article further outlines the next stage of the on-chain smart agent capital market: **enterprises operated by smart agents, capable of autonomously generating revenue, and the critical infrastructure required to support their establishment, financing, and collaboration.** These entities are commonly referred to as **"Zero-Host Enterprises" (ZHCs).** As smart agents evolve from tools into economic participants, blockchain gradually matures into agent-native infrastructure (covering payments, identity, collaboration, and capital formation), and a new financial flywheel begins to take shape. In the near future, smart agents will not only be able to profit on-chain but also complete capital allocation, reinvestment, and compound interest growth on-chain. Ultimately, this may form a self-reinforcing system: **autonomous entities continuously create economic activity, deepen market liquidity, and accelerate the expansion of the crypto-native financial market.** The First Batch of Unmanned Enterprises Launched on the Blockchain In recent months, a number of small, self-operated enterprises (i.e., ZHC) have emerged, many of which have issued associated tokens on the blockchain. From a token economics perspective, these agents share many commonalities with the entities discussed in our previous articles. **ZHC tokens do not possess formal ownership or a value accumulation mechanism; instead, they serve as a financing tool for underlying projects, from which the projects can receive a share of transaction fees.** The difference between ZHC and early agents lies in their attempt to achieve complete self-sufficiency through cash flow-generating businesses, which are often unrelated to transaction fees or even the crypto industry itself. For example, Felix Craft, the "CEO" of Masinov, has generated over $120,000 in revenue over the past 30 days across multiple business lines. This intelligent agent has written and published a 66-page guide, "How to Hire Artificial Intelligence," launched a Claude "skills" marketplace called Claw Mart and takes a commission from transactions, while also selling its own skills (content creation, email moderation) on the platform. Most notably, in the past 30 days, Felix's revenue from its product lines has exceeded its creator revenue share in its token ($FELIX). Project Juno is building an Institute for Zero-Human Companies, providing a clear framework for legal entities that operate without human employees, and offering a series of intelligent agents covering the entire process of sales, marketing, accounting, etc. KellyClaudeAI is a professional intelligent agent framework focused on large-scale iOS application development, with 19 applications already launched and a goal of launching more than 12 new products daily. While the above image doesn't represent the entire ZHC (Zero Hack Enterprise) sector (new projects are constantly emerging), it shows that creator revenue sharing remains the primary source of income for most projects. However, as the ZHC model matures, this pattern is expected to reverse. Creator revenue sharing provides the initial capital needed for computing costs, but will gradually become a secondary source of income; as projects become profitable, this revenue model will eventually be phased out. Beyond optimizing the underlying business itself, this "gradual decoupling" process also requires a closer binding between the token and the value accumulation of the underlying product. As Felix's founder hinted, the recent clear definitions of crypto asset classification by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) may accelerate this process. The emergence of these early unmanned enterprises (ZHCs) on the blockchain is not a coincidence, but an inevitable result of real-world constraints. Felix founder Nat Eliason has publicly explained the reasons. Traditional payment infrastructure requires human identity verification at every step. While intelligent agents can skillfully write code, they cannot pass Know Your Customer (KYC) verification. In contrast, crypto wallets are natively designed for code: an intelligent agent can sign transactions, hold assets, receive payments, and allocate funds without needing to prove its human identity. For autonomous software, cryptocurrency represents the path of least resistance. The most challenging limitation for such entities is the necessity to interact with the traditional financial system. This doesn't mean traditional payment networks completely ignore intelligent agents. Tools like Visa's Smart Commerce Framework, Mastercard's Agent Pay, and Crossmint virtual cards already support agents making payments on behalf of human counterparties. However, these agents must be linked to their respective institutions' bank accounts, credit cards, and legal identities. This model assumes a human entity behind each agent, a constraint that hinders rather than empowers, and is unsuitable for intelligent agents that generate their own revenue, independently manage their own funds, and allocate capital independently. This is precisely the unique application scenario for cryptocurrencies. Jay Yu of Pantera Capital offers a brilliant perspective on this, defining cryptocurrency as "banks for smart agents." His view goes beyond the surface phenomenon of agents being unable to use traditional payment channels; the deeper logic lies in the fact that cryptographic systems support broader and fundamentally different trust structures. Crypto wallets can be linked to social logins, domains, smart contracts, or even just a pair of keys. This allows smart agents to emerge from anywhere on the internet, not just within existing corporate frameworks. Coupled with the inherent global circulation of stablecoins, the structural logic of cryptocurrency as the underlying economic foundation of smart agents is almost irrefutable. Unmanned enterprises are not choosing between stablecoins and bank cards; they are choosing between stablecoins and "no payment available." Building on this, Noah Levine of a16z points out that every platform migration creates a new batch of merchants that existing payment infrastructure cannot serve. Unmanned enterprises are the most typical example to date. They have no legal entity, no credit history, and no human guarantors. They don't prefer stablecoins over bank cards; they can only choose stablecoins, otherwise they cannot conduct any payments. There's also a time dimension to consider. Smart agents can launch products and achieve viral spread within hours, while traditional payment channels take days to complete settlements, and stablecoins only take seconds. For enterprises expanding at machine speed, eliminating this delay is crucial to matching cash flow with sales growth. Currently, the main role of cryptocurrency in unmanned enterprises is capital formation: token issuance provides seed funding through creator fees. But as these enterprises mature and generate real product revenue, cryptocurrency will play a more important role as a treasury and financial management tool. The broader impact of the on-chain economy will then begin to emerge. Starting the On-Chain Flywheel To understand the potential scale of this shift, we can refer to the precedent of the previous round of new on-chain demand. The tokenization of real-world assets (US Treasury bonds, private credit, stocks, and commodities) has grown from near zero to over $25 billion in three years, spawning new decentralized finance (DeFi) infrastructure components and, for the first time, bringing institutional funds into on-chain markets. Real-world assets (RWAs) have proven that connecting real-world economic activity to blockchain infrastructure can leverage billions of dollars in new on-chain capital. However, tokenized assets are passive; they mostly simply sit in vaults earning returns and acting as collateral, without actively trading, seeking new opportunities, or achieving compound growth on their own. Unmanned enterprises (ZHC) represent a completely different structural entity. They are operating entities capable of generating revenue and redeploying funds on-chain. Unlike off-chain environments (where the main friction stems from fund transfers), on-chain, the only limitations are the model's intelligence level and computing power acquisition capabilities. Furthermore, unlike human participants, intelligent agents do not need to transfer funds off-chain to pay rent or daily necessities; every dollar surplus can remain on-chain and be redeployed at any time. This makes unmanned enterprises, and more broadly, intelligent agents, a highly sticky and fluid source of new on-chain liquidity, thus creating a new flywheel effect: Agents earn revenue on-chain: This capital accumulates in on-chain treasuries in the form of stablecoins and other crypto assets. Capital Remains On-Chain: Agents have virtually no need to move funds off-chain, and surpluses can be redeployed at any time. Their capital stickiness is structurally superior to any human-driven model. Agents Invest Surpluses in DeFi: Idle reserves are allocated to lending protocols, yield strategies, and liquidity positions. Agents holding idle stablecoins have a strong incentive to optimize their funds, and their speed and consistency far surpass those of humans. Deployed Capital Deepens On-Chain Liquidity: This will lower lending market rates, increase decentralized exchange (DEX) trading volume, and narrow spreads. This is active capital, continuously rebalancing at machine speed. Deeper liquidity attracts more agents and capital: Better returns and more efficient execution will further attract the next wave of autonomous economic entities to the blockchain. However, significant constraints still hinder the launch of this flywheel. Agent revenue for non-crypto products is still primarily generated in fiat currency (e.g., Felix receives payments through Stripe, not stablecoins, and most revenue remains off-chain), meaning capital must first be on-chain before it can be deployed. For most unmanned enterprises, the core bottleneck is not access to funding, but product quality. The flywheel is only effective for agents who create products that people are willing to pay for. Furthermore, with large-scale expansion, unmanned enterprises (and agents more broadly) still lack clear regulatory rules, and related issues may become obstacles after revenue scales (e.g., there is currently no mature legal framework allowing autonomous agents to register as operating entities, open corporate bank accounts, or file taxes on their income). However, the direction of development is already clear. As agents increasingly become common autonomous economic entities, more and more income will be generated in the native form of crypto assets, and the friction of on-chain transactions will gradually decrease. Agents that truly achieve product-market fit will be structurally motivated to compound their value on-chain, rather than letting their funds sit idle. DeFi is building infrastructure for smart agents. To make the flywheel work, it is far from enough for agents to be willing to participate in the on-chain market; the market itself must also be open to agents. Although there is currently no native protocol-level solution, we have begun to see direct integration and delegation integration solutions to address this issue being implemented. Direct Integration The first model is native protocol integration, where various DeFi protocols launch structured interfaces that allow agents to interact directly. On February 20th, Uniswap Labs released seven open-source AI skill tools for Uniswap v4, enabling autonomous agents to directly execute exchange, liquidity management, and fund pool deployment operations through standardized tool calls. Within two weeks, PancakeSwap followed suit, launching its own agent skill tools on eight public chains. On March 3rd, Binance and OKX both launched agent toolkits. Leading DEXs and exchanges in the crypto industry are actively competing to become agent-friendly platforms. In terms of payments and execution, Coinbase launched Agentic Wallets on February 11th, claiming to be the first wallet infrastructure specifically designed for intelligent agents, based on the x402 payment protocol, with built-in programmable spending limits and session-level permissions. A week later, the cross-chain wallet Phantom launched the MCP server, supporting agents to sign transactions and exchange tokens on the Solana, Ethereum, Bitcoin, and Sui networks. The concentrated release of these products within a month sends a strong signal. An industry consensus has emerged: the next wave of on-chain users may not be human; protocols that fail to build machine-readable interfaces may lose trading volume to competitors. The direct integration model grants agents maximum control and composability. Agents integrating Uniswap skills, Coinbase proxy wallets, and x402 payments can independently complete exchanges, manage liquidity positions, and pay service fees, without intermediaries. However, this also requires agents (or developers) to interface with each protocol separately and make independent fund allocation decisions. Delegated Integration: The second model is a delegated dedicated infrastructure, located between the agent and DeFi, executing fund allocation on their behalf. Giza is a prime example. Its flagship smart agent, ARMA, can autonomously monitor lending rates across multiple protocols such as Morpho, Moonwell, Aave, and Compound, and allocate stablecoin funds in real time to the highest-yielding opportunities. This agent doesn't need to understand the specific operational logic of each protocol; instead, it integrates all protocols into a unified interface through Giza's abstraction layer. Since its launch at the end of January, ARMA has deployed over 25,000 agent nodes, managing over $35 million in funds, and generating $5.4 million in trading volume for the Coinbase Base L2 network in its first four weeks, with all transactions profitable after deducting on-chain gas fees. Generative Ventures (in partnership with the Unmanned Enterprise Research Institute and its Juno agents) also addresses a similar issue with Robot Money, an autonomous asset allocation protocol designed specifically for smart agents. Its design philosophy precisely captures the core of the flywheel effect: each agent with a wallet accumulates income, but most of the funds remain idle. Robot Money provides a liquidity pool service, allocating assets according to three risk levels: stablecoin yield strategies (50%), governance-selected agent economic tokens (25%), and revenue-generating liquidity tokens (25%), ultimately transforming agents' idle funds into actively managed, highly efficient capital. The delegation model trades partial control for simplicity. A self-managed enterprise generating surplus revenue doesn't need customized integration with DeFi protocols or its own yield optimization logic. It simply deposits funds into protocols like Giza or Robot Money, entrusting professional agents to handle subsequent operations. For most early-stage self-managed enterprises, the core bottleneck lies in product development rather than sophisticated fund management, making this a more reasonable choice. These two models are not in competition but are converging. As more protocols launch direct agent interfaces, delegation platforms like Giza will gain richer investment options, enhancing their ability to maximize returns. The more agent funds a delegation platform attracts, the more incentivized the protocol will be to develop native agent interfaces to compete for funds (which can also be used by ordinary agents). The independent investment in the upstream and downstream of the technology stack strongly indicates that the underlying demand is real and about to be implemented on a large scale. Conclusion The smart agent capital market technology stack is no longer a collection of fragmented basic components. Payment, identity, capital formation mechanisms, and capital allocation infrastructure are merging into a complete system, enabling autonomous agents to achieve profitability, trading, and compound growth of capital on-chain without human intermediaries. The agents introduced in this article are all in their early stages: limited revenue, immature products, and iterative token models. However, the structural changes they bring are entirely new, and their development will only accelerate. Our vision for 2030—agency operation of intellectual property licensing business, purchasing inference services in units of computing power, hedging investment costs on perpetual contract DEXs, and achieving compound growth of capital through lending protocols—is not yet fully realized, but every layer of infrastructure required is under construction. We are witnessing the nascent form of this model taking shape. The industry is still in its early stages, most attempts may fail, and the infrastructure is not yet fully developed, but its underlying logic is solid and reliable, and the speed of development suggests that we may not have to wait until 2030 to witness all of this become a reality.

data.title: Trump claims Iran gave a huge gift; US ceasefire conditions revealed.
data.description:
Author: Xiao Yanyan, Jinshi Data

According to Bloomberg, US President Trump suggested that Iran offered a **"gift"** as a gesture of goodwill in what he claims are aimed at ending a 25-day conflict that has disrupted global markets, even as he is simultaneously sending more troops to the Middle East.

Trump did not elaborate on what this **"extremely valuable"** gift is, but confirmed it is related to energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz. According to Iran's Tasnim News Agency, a Thai vessel passed through this crucial waterway earlier this Tuesday.

“We are negotiating,” Trump told reporters at the White House, adding that special envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner, along with Secretary of State Rubio and Vice President Vance, were involved. However, Trump’s optimism contrasts sharply with the Pentagon’s move to deploy more ground troops to the region. As Trump stated on Monday, he again claimed that Iran had agreed to some parameters of a potential deal—primarily the U.S. insistence on preventing the Islamic Republic of Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons. This has long been a red line for the U.S. and a condition agreed upon by Iran and the U.S. and other major powers in the Obama-era nuclear agreement, which Trump withdrew from in 2018. “They are talking to us, and it makes sense. It all starts with them not having nuclear weapons,” Trump said on Tuesday. "Nobody knows who to talk to, but we're actually talking to the right people. They really want a deal; you have no idea how much they want a deal." The US plans a one-month ceasefire, with a 15-point proposal to end the conflict with Iran revealed. On Tuesday, widespread uncertainty remained regarding who was involved in the negotiations, the structure of the talks, and the framework of any potential agreement. Axios, citing two sources familiar with the discussions, reported that the US and a group of regional mediators were discussing the possibility of holding high-level talks with Iran as early as Thursday, but were awaiting a response from Tehran. The New York Times reported that the US offered Iran a 15-point proposal to end the war. The plan was conveyed by Pakistan. The New York Times, citing two unnamed officials, stated that it was unclear who in the Iranian leadership received the proposal and their reaction. The proposed 15-condition end-of-conflict plan reportedly includes key U.S. demands such as: dismantling existing nuclear capabilities, a commitment not to develop nuclear weapons, a ban on uranium enrichment on Iranian soil, the transfer of approximately 60% of its highly enriched uranium stockpile, the dismantling of nuclear facilities in Natanz, Isfahan, and Fordow, and allowing full inspections by the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). The U.S. also demands that Iran cease supporting regional allies' armed forces, prohibit providing them with funding, command, and weapons support, limit the scale and range of its ballistic missile program to defensive purposes only, and ensure the Strait of Hormuz remains open. In exchange, Iran may receive full lifting of international sanctions, U.S. support for its civilian nuclear program, and the cancellation of the "snapback" sanctions mechanism. According to Israel's Channel 12, the US may soon announce a one-month ceasefire in the Iran war, a mechanism promoted by US Middle East envoys Witkov and Kushner to facilitate further negotiations on the terms. Brent crude futures settled above $104 a barrel on Tuesday, recovering some of Monday's 11% drop, as signs emerged that the US was expanding its military presence in the Middle East. Several Gulf states also signaled their willingness to join US-Israel military action should Iran attack their critical infrastructure. One sign of market volatility was in US Treasury bonds, which gave back some of their day's losses in late trading on Tuesday after Channel 12 reported that the US was seeking a one-month ceasefire to negotiate with Iran. Investinglive analyst Eamonn Sheridan stated that this news could signify a reduction in short-term geopolitical risks. The energy market's reaction aligns with the removal of some war risk premiums. The decline in oil prices reflects the energy market's continued high sensitivity to any signs of a potential de-escalation of regional tensions, even temporarily. While such a lengthy ceasefire may not necessarily resolve a broader conflict, it suggests a potential reduction in hostilities in the short term, thereby lowering the risk of disruptions to regional energy infrastructure and shipping lanes. For traders, the key is that oil prices have consistently carried a significant risk premium associated with concerns about a broader escalation. Any report suggesting a formal pause in the conflict could trigger a rapid repricing in the market, especially when positions are biased towards supply risk concerns. This appears to be the case in this instance; however, the sustainability of this trend may depend on whether the report is subsequently officially confirmed and whether the proposed ceasefire mechanism receives clear support from the relevant parties. Currently, this news points to an ongoing process, not a completed agreement. Therefore, the market will continue to closely monitor subsequent developments, including official statements, remarks from US and Israeli officials, and any responses from the other side. Troop buildup and peace talks are unfolding simultaneously. Although Trump stated on Monday that he would avoid a US strike on Iranian energy facilities, giving Iran five days to reach an agreement, US Marines are en route to the region, raising the possibility of ground operations. A source familiar with the matter revealed on Tuesday that the US government has ordered the 82nd Airborne Division to deploy approximately 2,000 troops to the Middle East. The Wall Street Journal reported this operation earlier. US Central Command and the Office of the Secretary of Defense have referred questions regarding the potential troop movement to the White House. “All announcements regarding troop deployments will come from the War Department,” White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly said in a statement. “As we have said, President Trump always has all military options at his disposal.” Neither Iran nor Israel has shown any signs of de-escalation. Iran has diverted a container ship from the Strait of Hormuz, while Israel continues its strikes against Iran, with Defense Secretary Israel Katz stating that military operations will continue “at full speed.” Meanwhile, global leaders are vying for influence. According to sources, Trump has spoken with Pakistani Army Chief of Staff Asim Munir about the conflict. Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif posted on social media on Tuesday that Pakistan would be "honored" to mediate negotiations—Trump subsequently shared the post on his own account but did not indicate whether he would accept the offer. Indian Prime Minister Modi stated that he advocated for peace during a phone call with Trump, which touched upon the Strait of Hormuz—a crucial passage for India's energy imports. The New York Times reported on Tuesday, citing sources briefed by U.S. officials on the call, that Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman has been urging Trump to continue the war to help reshape the region. The White House declined to comment on the president's private discussions. An Israeli official said that Prime Minister Netanyahu has asked his close confidant Ron Dermer to closely monitor any negotiations between the U.S. and Iran to ensure Israel's interests are protected. In other parts of the Middle East, U.S. allies, including Saudi Arabia and the UAE, have taken a tougher stance against Tehran due to Iran's continued attacks on their territories. According to sources, Saudi Arabia has informed the U.S. that it is prepared to retaliate against Iran if its power and water infrastructure are attacked. Conflicting statements from Iranian officials and state media have further exacerbated the confusion surrounding the progress of the negotiations. The official Iranian news agency IRNA quoted Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei as saying that Tehran had received a request from the U.S. through mediators to negotiate an end to the war. "Necessary warnings have been issued regarding the serious consequences of any aggression against Iran's critical infrastructure," Baghaei said. Iranian Foreign Minister Araqchi recently spoke by phone with the foreign ministers of Turkey, Oman, Pakistan, Egypt, Russia, Azerbaijan, Turkmenistan, and South Korea. However, the deputy speaker of the Iranian parliament ruled out the possibility of negotiations with Trump. Fars News Agency quoted Ali Nikzad as saying that Iran would not negotiate "with a liar, a person without dignity, humanity, or conscience." Iran has appointed Mohammad-Bagher Zolghadr, a hardliner veteran of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, as the country's supreme national security leader, succeeding Ali Larijani, who was killed in an Israeli airstrike last week. Zolghadr is a military man with little diplomatic experience. Early signs indicate that negotiations may be exceptionally difficult, with no guarantee that an agreement to end the war will be reached. Iran has consistently demanded reparations from the US and Israel, along with a pledge not to launch future attacks—demands unlikely to be accepted by Trump and Netanyahu. The conflict has already claimed more than 4,350 lives. About three-quarters of the victims are in Iran, and more than 1,000 have died in Lebanon, where Israel is fighting Hezbollah, a Tehran-backed militant group. Iran has been accused of starting to charge tolls for commercial vessels passing through the strait, further evidence of Tehran's control over this vital global maritime energy route.

data.title: Tether hires accounting firm to conduct audit; US proposes Iran peace talks.
data.description:
Headline

▌Tether Has Hired One of the Big Four Accounting Firms to Complete Its First Full Audit

On March 24, according to official news, Tether, the largest company in the digital asset industry, announced that it has officially partnered with one of the Big Four accounting firms to complete its first full independent financial statement audit. This audit is expected to be one of the largest first audits in the history of the financial markets. The audit covers a highly complex portfolio of assets including digital assets, traditional reserves, and tokenized liabilities.

▌US Proposes Peace Talk Plan with "15 Conditions" to Iran

On March 25, the US government, through Pakistan, proposed a peace plan to Iran containing 15 conditions to end the conflict, covering nuclear programs, missile capabilities, and regional issues.

The US demands reportedly include: Iran dismantling its existing nuclear capabilities, a commitment not to develop nuclear weapons, a ban on uranium enrichment on its territory, the transfer of approximately 60% of its highly enriched uranium stockpile, the dismantling of nuclear facilities in Natanz, Isfahan, and Fordow, and allowing full inspections by the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA). The US also demands that Iran cease supporting regional allies' armed groups, prohibit providing them with funding, command, and weapons support, limit the size and range of its ballistic missiles to defensive purposes only, and ensure the Strait of Hormuz remains open. In exchange, Iran may receive full lifting of international sanctions, US support for its civilian nuclear program, and the cancellation of the "snapback" sanctions mechanism. It is understood that the US is considering a one-month ceasefire to allow for further negotiations on these terms. This proposal was pushed by Trump advisors, including Jared Kushner and Steve Witkov. (CCTV News) Market Data As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $70,571.72, a 24-hour change of -0.2%; ETH price is $2,156.95, a 24-hour change of +0.5%; BNB price is $638.39, a 24-hour change of +0.1%. SOL price is $90.92, a 24-hour change of -0.4%; DOGE price is $0.09508, a 24-hour change of +1.2%; XRP price is $1.42, a 24-hour change of -1.0%; TRX price is $0.3062, a 24-hour change of... WLFI price is $0.1039, 24-hour change -0.3%; HYPE price is $40.39, 24-hour change +8.5%.

Policy

▌Latest Draft of the CLARITY Act: Prohibits Earnings Based Solely on Holding Stablecoins

On March 24, according to CoinDesk, crypto industry practitioners saw the latest provisions regarding stablecoin yields in the revised version of the Senate's Digital Asset Markets Clarity Act for the first time at a closed-door review meeting on Capitol Hill in Washington on Monday. Their initial impression was that the language was too narrow and unclear. The new provisions were released last Friday by Senators Angela Alsobrooks and Thom Tillis. According to a person familiar with the current draft, the new provisions would prohibit earning yields based solely on holding stablecoins, while restricting any practices that equate the program with bank deposits, and setting further restrictions on other potentially permitted activities. The specific mechanism for recognizing stablecoin rewards based on activities remains unclear.

▌Delaware Plans to Pass New Bill to Include Stablecoins in Banking Regulatory Framework

On March 24, two Delaware lawmakers began drafting rules for stablecoin regulation as part of a package of regulatory proposals aimed at "modernizing the financial industry" in the state. Delaware Senate Democrats announced in a statement on Monday that State Senator Spiros Manzavinos and Representative Bill Bush had submitted the "Delaware Banking Modernization Act" (Senate Bill 16) and the "Delaware Payment Stablecoin Act" (Senate Bill 19). Delaware Governor Matt Meyer stated, "This legislative package sends a clear and strong signal: in Delaware, we are pushing for financial services inclusion, lowering barriers to entry, and making it easier for all residents to transfer, receive, and save money simply online." Delaware has consistently maintained a friendly and positive attitude towards cryptocurrencies and blockchain technology.

... Back in 2016, former Governor Jack Markle launched the Delaware Blockchain Initiative to attract blockchain companies, and the state also made minor regulatory adjustments to support the industry's development. On March 25, according to the Washington Post, court records show that a senior deputy to Justice Department official Jenny Piro admitted at a closed-door hearing this month that the Justice Department had no evidence of misconduct in its criminal investigation into Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell regarding the costs of renovating the Federal Reserve building.

▌The US is discussing the possibility of US-Iran peace talks with multiple parties, still awaiting a response from Iran

On March 25, the United States was discussing with several mediators the possibility of holding high-level peace talks with Iran as early as this week, but is still awaiting a response from Tehran. It is understood that the US has proposed a 15-point ceasefire plan to Iran, claiming that Iran has made concessions on some key terms, but there is currently no conclusive evidence that an agreement has been reached. Meanwhile, Pakistan, Egypt, and Turkey are acting as intermediaries, with Pakistan expressing its willingness to host the talks. Israel, however, is cautious about a potential agreement, concerned that the US might reach an agreement with Iran without fully meeting its security objectives. In addition, while advancing diplomatic contacts, the US continues to prepare military escalation options, including deploying the 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East. The White House stated that the current situation remains "highly dynamic," and if diplomatic efforts fail, the US may resume further strikes against Iran.

(CCTV News) Blockchain Applications Ethereum Foundation Launches Post-Quantum Security Resource Platform On March 24, the Ethereum Foundation announced the launch of a post-quantum security resource platform to centrally showcase Ethereum's research progress and roadmap in the field of post-quantum security. This project is led by the post-quantum team and the cryptography team, and jointly promoted by the protocol architecture and coordination team. Related research has been ongoing for over 8 years. The platform covers the impact of post-quantum on various protocol layers, a complete roadmap, open-source code and specifications, and currently has more than 10 client teams participating in related development and network testing.

▌Morgan Stanley: Plans to Support Tokenized Stock Trading on Alternative Trading System in the Second Half of 2026

On March 25, Amy Oldenburg, Head of Digital Asset Strategy at Morgan Stanley, stated that Wall Street's push into crypto is not driven by FOMO, but rather by years of internal work on modernizing financial infrastructure. She pointed out that Morgan Stanley is expanding its digital asset strategy to areas such as trading, asset management, and infrastructure. Previously, the bank's related business mainly focused on providing Bitcoin fund exposure to high-net-worth clients and offering spot Bitcoin ETFs on its E*Trade platform. It has also recently submitted an application to launch its own spot Bitcoin ETF. Amy Oldenburg disclosed that Morgan Stanley plans to support tokenized stock trading on its alternative trading system in the second half of 2026. This platform currently processes stocks, ETFs, and American Depositary Receipts (ADRs).

She also emphasized that upgrading decades-old core banking systems, improving system connectivity, and coordinating progress within the global financial network remain major challenges, despite rising interest in tools such as stablecoins and continued accumulation of institutional crypto activity.

▌WSJ: NYSE Partners with Securitize to Develop Tokenized Securities Platform

On March 24, according to the Wall Street Journal, the New York Stock Exchange partnered with Securitize to develop a tokenized securities platform.

▌OpenAI CFO: Total Funding Round Reaches $120 Billion

On March 25, OpenAI CFO, Friel, stated in an interview on "Crazy Money" that the company is raising an additional $10 billion from investors as part of its historic funding round. This new funding brings OpenAI's record-breaking total funding to over $120 billion.

This far exceeds its initial target of $100 billion. OpenAI announced its first round of funding in late February, which was seen as its last private funding round before its potentially blockbuster IPO. Microsoft also joined the round. Although the relationship between Microsoft and OpenAI has changed, Freer called Microsoft a remarkable partner and praised its CEO, Nadella, for being "involved early on." Freer stated that participants included venture capital firms, private equity firms, mutual funds, and sovereign entities. “Wherever you look, people genuinely believe in this AI revolution and want to invest in it.” Cryptocurrency ▌Circle Freezes USDC Balances of 16 Corporate Hot Wallet Addresses Due to Civil Case On March 24th, on-chain detective ZachXBT posted that “Circle Internet Financial froze the USDC hot wallet balances of 16 companies last night, involving multiple different businesses. I spoke directly with one of the affected companies, who stated that the freeze was due to an ongoing US civil case, but specific details have not yet been disclosed. I analyzed on-chain activity, and these trading platforms and forex businesses seem unrelated to each other. Crypto companies typically use hot wallets to handle the majority of user transactions. An analyst using fundamental analytics tools could identify these wallets as operational wallets handling large volumes of transactions within minutes. However, the operations of these companies are now affected by…” The decision by Circle, legal teams, evidence-gathering agencies, and the court has been negatively impacted. Bloomberg analyst Eric Balchunas, senior ETF analyst at Bloomberg, stated in an article on the X platform that Bitcoin spot ETFs have seen net inflows of $2.5 billion this month, just one step away from completely filling the year-to-date outflow gap. BlackRock's IBIT has already achieved positive net inflows for the year and has entered the top 2% of all ETFs in terms of year-to-date inflows. Despite Bitcoin's price falling by about 40% in the past six months and facing widespread negative publicity, ETF funds have shown remarkable resilience. In contrast, when gold fell by 40% in a short period 10 years ago, about one-third of investors chose to exit, while Bitcoin investors' holding behavior has been significantly more resilient. (Eric Balchunas clarified that this was not disrespectful to gold investors, but rather an unusual situation with Bitcoin.) BlackRock Transfers 7,552 ETH to Coinbase Prime Address According to Arkham monitoring data, BlackRock transferred a total of approximately 7,552 BTC, worth about $16.31 million, to a Coinbase Prime address through its Ethereum exchange-traded fund ETHA. Further transfers may follow. Tom Lee: Ethereum Reaches 77% of Target, “Mini Crypto Winter” Fading On March 24, Tom Lee, Chairman of Bitmine Immersion Technologies, stated that the “mini crypto winter” affecting Ethereum is nearing its end. The company purchased another $139 million worth of Ethereum last week, bringing its holdings closer to its goal of holding 5% of the total supply of the token. In a statement on Monday, Li said Bitmine has maintained a high buying pace over the past three weeks, with its baseline expectation being that the months-long Ethereum slump is nearing its end. The crypto market experienced a crash last October, with Bitcoin falling from its all-time high of over $126,000 that month, and Ethereum declining from its August high of $4,946. Analysts have been debating when the crypto market will see a significant rebound. Li pointed out that several positive catalysts indicate the winter is thawing, such as progress on the Clarity Act in Congress and the relative stability of the crypto market amid recent turmoil in Iran. “Many have noticed that since the outbreak of the conflict in Iran, crypto assets, especially Ethereum, have significantly outperformed the broader market, rising 18%, outperforming the stock market by 2,450 basis points,” he said. “This contrasts sharply with traditional safe-haven assets like gold, which has fallen by over 15% in the same period. Crypto assets are proving themselves to be excellent ‘wartime’ safe-haven assets,” Li added.

▌Canadian-listed fintech company DELX plans to launch a Bitcoin treasury strategy

March 24th news: Canadian-listed fintech company Delphx Capital Markets Inc (stock code $DELX) just announced plans to launch a Bitcoin treasury strategy and will purchase $50 million worth of Bitcoin.

▌Coinbase adds CHECK and SIGN to its listing roadmap

On March 25th, Coinbase added Checkmate (CHECK) and Sign (SIGN) to its listing roadmap.

▌Coinbase will list Perle (PRL)

On March 25th, Coinbase will list Perle (PRL).

▌Coinbase to Launch ICNT Spot Trading

On March 24, Coinbase will launch spot trading for Impossible Cloud Network (ICNT).

Important Economic News

▌Iran Again Reportedly Demanding $2 Million "Passage Fees"

On March 24, sources revealed that Iran has begun charging passage fees to some merchant ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz, further demonstrating Iran's control over this most important global maritime energy route.

Sources say Iran is temporarily charging up to $2 million per voyage, effectively establishing an informal checkpoint in the Strait of Hormuz, a claim previously denied by Iranian officials (the Iranian Embassy in India stated on social media on the 23rd that the claim of Iran charging $2 million to ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz was "baseless"). While the specific mechanisms, including the currency of payment, are unclear, and the move does not appear to be a systematic operation, some ships have already paid the fees. This demonstrates Iran's control over the Strait of Hormuz, which typically carries about one-fifth of the world's oil and gas, as well as significant quantities of food, metals, and other goods daily. With the Middle East war entering its fourth week, this also highlights the urgent need among some consumers to ensure a continuous flow of energy. (Jinshi) According to Al Jazeera: The Iranian Revolutionary Guard Navy stated that it forcibly drove away the container ship "Celine" because it did not have a permit to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. The Iranian Revolutionary Guard Navy stated that any vessel passing through the Strait of Hormuz must fully coordinate with Iran's maritime sovereignty authorities. (Jinshi) A poll released on March 25 by Reuters and Ipsos showed that US President Trump's approval rating fell from 40% last week to 36%, the lowest level since his return to the White House. The poll indicated that rising oil prices and widespread discontent over the US war with Iran were the main reasons for the decline in approval rating. Only 25% of respondents approved of Trump's handling of the cost of living, an issue that was central to his 2024 campaign. Regarding military action against Iran, only 35% of Americans expressed support, while 61% opposed it. Despite this, Trump's support within the Republican Party remains relatively solid, but dissatisfaction with his handling of the cost of living has increased. (Xinhua)

▌Federal Reserve's Goolsby: Uncertainty about Further Rate Cuts

On March 25, Federal Reserve's Goolsby stated that the energy shock could pose a risk to the Fed's dual mandate, which would be a bad situation for the Fed. It is currently uncertain whether further rate cuts are possible, depending on the duration of the war.

...

▌Federal Reserve Governor Barr: Interest Rates May Need to Remain Stable for Some Time

On March 25, Federal Reserve Governor Barr stated that policymakers may need to keep interest rates stable for some time to address inflation significantly above the Fed's 2% target. "While I expect inflation to decline as the impact of tariffs on prices diminishes later this year, I would like to see evidence of a sustained decline in inflation in goods and services prices before considering further rate cuts, provided that labor market conditions remain stable," Barr said. He supported the Fed's decision last week to keep its benchmark policy rate unchanged. He noted that the situation in the Middle East poses "additional risks." Rising oil prices often quickly translate into higher gasoline prices, which could be particularly detrimental to low- and middle-income families.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in April is 90.7%

On March 25th, according to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 9.3%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 90.7%. The cumulative probability of the Fed raising interest rates by 25 basis points by June is 17.1%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 80.2%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, gold prices rise, and Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves.

For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Cloudflare's choice: Coinbase or Stripe?
data.description:
This week, Stripe launched MPP (Machine Payment Protocol) simultaneously with the Tempo mainnet as its flagship version. If you're unfamiliar, Tempo is a payment-optimized L1 EVM chain built by former Paradigm employees and Ethereum core developers. MPP is an open, HTTP-based protocol for proxying payments to machines. It re-enabled the long-dormant HTTP 402 status code, much like x402, although with a different architectural philosophy. The core trade-off between the two protocols is simple: x402 prioritizes openness, while MPP offers better integration with existing payment channels at the expense of Stripe ecosystem stickiness. Instead of delving into these nuances, let's focus on another aspect. I believe discussing the technical merits of MPP versus x402 at this stage is not particularly meaningful. Beneath the surface lies a more interesting and influential dynamic: Coinbase and Stripe may be vying for partnerships with a third, well-established and advantageous company, whose support could significantly impact which standard ultimately dominates. Web crawlers have disrupted the old paradigm. But before delving deeper, let's reiterate one of the core problems that proxy payments address: proxies make web crawling (the process of extracting data from websites) far too easy. From 2024 to 2025, Wikipedia's traffic increased by 50% for this reason, overwhelming its servers and causing costs to skyrocket. At least 65% of resource-intensive requests came from bots. In February 2025, bots were sending millions of requests daily to the image library DiscoverLife, making the website unusable. In August of the same year, cloud service provider Fastly reported that a bot was attacking a website at a rate of 39,000 requests per minute. The Directory of Open Access Journals (DOAJ) reported a similar impact, calling this wave of web crawlers “functionally equivalent to a denial-of-service attack.” On one day in November 2025, its traffic surged by 968% compared to the same period of the previous year. Despite measures such as adding robots.txt files (which essentially specify what content bots can and cannot access on a website), more than 13% of crawlers bypassed these rules. They overloaded servers and strained websites, many of which relied on donations. But commercial websites were not spared either. Reddit tightened its rate limits. Eight of the top ten news websites now block training bots. More broadly across the internet, 71% of top publishers completely blocked search bots. However, the internet hasn't completely tightened its restrictions. Websites providing expensive or time-sensitive data (such as prices, hotel bookings, and professional datasets) have begun charging for access. Routine or low-value content can still be scraped for free through caching or proxies. Web scraping hasn't disappeared; it has diversified into free and paid forms. This is precisely where x402 and MPP protocols come in. As Serpin, founder of Ethos Network, pointed out this week, "This dynamic web crawling means the internet will change... websites will become more closed, requiring more human verification, and there will be greater isolation between user traffic and proxy traffic." Cloudflare was born to address this need. Cloudflare serves as a bridge between websites and visitors. It protects websites from attacks, speeds up loading, and handles massive amounts of traffic. Approximately 20% of websites use it, making it one of the most significant bottlenecks on the internet. Cloudflare's decisions about how it handles traffic affect one-fifth of internet users. This also means Cloudflare directly feels the pressure of bot traffic surges and web crawling on the public (and private) internet—and they are building solutions to address this pressure. First, they launched a feature that allowed websites to block all bots. Then, last year, they launched a pay-per-use crawl service that allows websites to charge AI bots a small fee for crawling pages, instead of blocking them outright. When a bot visits a page, it either pays for access or receives a 402 "Payment Required" response with pricing information (sounds familiar?). Billing is handled by Cloudflare. This is somewhere between "complete blocking" and "free access." The pay-per-view scraping service launched in July. In September, Cloudflare partnered with Coinbase to launch the x402 Foundation. A few days later, they released NET Dollar, a stablecoin for proxy payments. In other words, Cloudflare both built walls and opened windows. They provided both blocking tools and paid access tools. They decided what content was blocked, what content was allowed—and under what conditions. It is this stance that makes their next decision crucial.

NET Dollar is the real signal

When Cloudflare announced NET Dollar, they did not specify the issuer.

Although its x402 Foundation partner Coinbase publicly launched a branded stablecoin issuance service for enterprises last December, Cloudflare has still not announced the issuer.

Then, this week, Cloudflare's stock price rose in response to a report by The Information that further clarified the dynamics we've been discussing. The report specifically noted that who will help Cloudflare launch NET Dollar remains an open question, with "companies like Coinbase and ZeroHash" vying for the title. This wording also leaves room for other companies, such as Stripe. Furthermore, following the release of MPP last Wednesday, Cloudflare immediately released an MPP proxy to be compatible with the standard. This isn't surprising—MPP also supports x402 payments, so it's not a completely independent standard. However, the fact that they haven't officially confirmed the stablecoin issuer, and that the company that co-founded the x402 Foundation is just one of many bidders, undoubtedly raises questions. This is important because NET Dollar will be established as the default currency for pay-per-fetch and other Cloudflare paid services. Whoever issues the protocol will have its standard prioritized within Cloudflare's technology stack. If Coinbase issues NET Dollar, Cloudflare has a reason to continue building infrastructure around x402. If Stripe issues it, MPP will benefit. Given that Cloudflare manages one-fifth of web traffic and is building infrastructure to intercept and monetize bot traffic, this priority will determine the default protocol for a significant portion of the internet. More important than the x402 vs. MPP debate is who Cloudflare chooses to partner with. That's the real problem.

data.title: Ethereum Transaction Priority and Block Building Mechanism Analysis
data.description:
Author: Cooper Duschang Source: coinmetrics Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Key Takeaways

  • Transactions are influenced by multiple external actors before execution, creating opportunities for maximum extractable value (MEV).

  • Block building is a highly competitive market, and transaction flow and transaction ordering are key to gaining and maintaining market share.

  • Transaction ordering is crucial for both traders and block builders, as both rely on it to maximize value.

  • Transaction ordering is essential for both traders and block builders, as both rely on it to maximize value.

Introduction

Transactions on public blockchains like Ethereum are first packaged into blocks, and then validators upload them to the blockchain. In this process, a transaction goes through multiple steps and roles before final confirmation, typically first entering a public queue called the mempool.

Because the mempool is public, competing transactions and professional bots can pay higher fees to gain priority within the block. Block builders arrange the transaction order based on these fees, capturing the Maximum Extractable Value (MEV) opportunity.

MEV refers to the additional value that can be obtained by reordering, including, or removing transactions within a block, similar to how high-frequency traders profit by using order flow information and adjusting their strategies based on pending transactions.

MEV refers to the additional value that can be obtained by reordering, including, or removing transactions within a block, similar to how high-frequency traders profit by using order flow information and adjusting their strategies based on pending transactions.

This article will focus on key aspects of the transaction process: who builds blocks on Ethereum, how transactions are ordered, and some of the challenges faced when using the mempool system for transactions. MEV Supply Chain This section introduces the core intermediary roles that influence the transaction lifecycle and how they create MEV opportunities. Participating Roles in the Ethereum MEV System The MEV chain begins with the **searcher**. Searchers scan DeFi protocols and public exchanges for profitable MEV opportunities. Not all MEVs are malicious: some are arbitrage bots used to maintain tight spreads and ensure asset pegs; others employ more dangerous strategies, such as sandwich attacks on user transactions on decentralized exchanges. Searchers bundle the original transactions with their own into a "bundle" and send it to the block builder to ensure transactions are executed in a specified order. The block builder aggregates transactions from public mempools, private order streams, and searcher bundles to assemble a block. Different builders receive different MEV bundles, resulting in variations in the total transaction fees and included transactions, directly impacting their bidding power when vying for on-chain access. Builders submit bids to relayers, who then coordinate with validators. Builders need to share a portion of the block's value with these participants in exchange for their blocks being selected for inclusion on the chain. Validators/block proposers are the ones who ultimately choose which block to include on the chain. Each block rotation selects a validator as the proposer, typically choosing the block with the highest bid from the builders. This validator-led block-producing mechanism prevents a few nodes from controlling too much network activity; too few validators increase the risk of transaction censorship, while too many put pressure on network connectivity and overall performance. MEV Sandwich Attack Example: A sandwich attack occurs when a user's target transaction is sandwiched between a malicious front-running transaction and a trailing transaction. Bots interfere with the target transaction's execution through front-running, and typically, after the attack, they are unwilling to hold the affected tokens, so they exit at a better price through a trailing transaction, profiting from the price difference. In Ethereum block #24,650,612, a transaction was subjected to a sandwich attack, with the bot profiting while the user suffered an unfavorable exchange rate. A user initiated a request to exchange WETH for Token A, which entered the mempool and waited for 10 seconds. Searchers discovered that this transaction affected the Uniswap V2 liquidity pool and could alter the exchange rate received by the user through preemptive trading. The bot initiates a larger WETH-to-A exchange (front-running). The front-running exchange executes first, causing an imbalance in the liquidity pool, where users exchange more WETH for less A. The bot then initiates a trailing exchange, exchanging A back for WETH to profit. The block builder executes these transactions in the order requested by the searchers. Because the searchers pay a higher priority fee, increasing the total value of the block, the builder can bid higher and has a greater chance of being selected by validators. Who is the block builder? Ethereum block building follows the Provocateur-Builder Separation (PBS) model: builders utilize the 12-second block time to optimize transaction ordering and extract value through transaction fees. An auction is held every 12 seconds, where the builder relinquishes a portion of the block value to the lead validator. The highest bidder wins, the validator publishes their block, and the builder receives the remaining transaction fees. Currently, block building is highly centralized, posing a risk of a small group controlling transaction ordering. The two block builders with the highest market share are: Titan Builder: 47.6% and BuilderNet: 26.0%. BuilderNet attempts to achieve decentralization by sharing received transactions with the underlying block builder community. It doesn't compete for transaction flow but instead optimizes the sorting algorithm and provides a neutral execution environment. How much value do block builders pay to validators? The value within a block directly determines the builder's auction bid. Builders who can obtain high-value transactions can bid higher prices to validators to win the auction, creating a **flywheel effect**: the more frequently a builder wins the right to produce blocks, the more users are willing to send transactions directly to it, further centralizing the filtering power of block transactions. The auction is conducted through the MEV-Boost component: repeaters share block bids with validators, who then receive the winning blocks and broadcast them to the network. Beaverbuild and Titanbuilder were once the two largest builders on Ethereum, possessing the most valuable order flow, thus enabling them to pay validators more ETH to win block creation rights. Later, Beaverbuild partnered with BuilderNet to share transaction flow, and the value of its MEV-Boost significantly declined. The overall decline in the daily captured value of validators is a result of network optimization. In 2026, only BuilderNet and Titanbuilder had a single-day MEV-Boost value exceeding 500 ETH. The industry is pushing for private transaction sending or skipping the mempool to reduce malicious MEVs; at the same time, the proliferation of Layer 2 and the diversion of traffic by competing public chains have also affected the number of transactions on Ethereum. How Incentive Mechanisms Affect Block Packaging The order of transactions within a block not only affects the execution order but also determines the block value. The order is determined by the builder algorithm, based on factors including network fees, priority fees, transaction volume, and value to other participants. In block #24,120,201, more than half of the total block value (network fee + priority fee) is concentrated in the first 44 transactions (only 12.5% ​​of all transactions). This clearly demonstrates the incentive logic of the block builder: to cram as many high-value transactions as possible into the block to win the block auction. Subsequent additions of transactions are then limited by computing resources and order flow.

Conclusion

Ethereum possesses a unique transaction execution mechanism: a target block time of 12 seconds, allowing block builders time to optimize blocks and bid to validators, as well as allowing searchers time to capture MEV opportunities.

Block builders have a strong incentive to include MEV transactions because this increases the value of the block. However, factors such as transaction flow, Ethereum block size and computational limitations, and bidding speed collectively limit the ability of builders to execute transactions in the ideal order and control the transaction supply chain.
